text:
Abstract:
Natural Language Processing is a fascinating topic in the field of AI. It has wide variety of application in the modern world. One of the famous applications of it is Sentiment Analysis. Sentiment Analysis means analyzing and interpreting the emotions behind the text  data. It is an important task in Natural Language Processing and is widely used in areas such as customer reviews, opinion mining, and recommendation systems. Sentiment analysis is done with the help of machine learning,

another subset of Artificial Intelligence. Many traditional machine learning models use a Bag-of-Words (technique used to convert text into numerical data) approach, which often removes common stopwords such as “is”, “the”,etc. during the preprocessing. However, this process also removes negation words like not, no and never.  Removing these words can change the meaning of a sentence and lead to incorrect sentiment prediction. For e.g. Before preprocessing – “Food is not good”, After preprocessi

ng- “Food good”,  here “is not ” is removed which changes the meaning of the text from negative to positive and the ml model makes the wrong prediction. In this chapter, we are going to see how keeping keeping the negation-related or negative words like no, not, can affect the accuracy of the classification models. As of advanced models like transformers architecture and BERT can easily do this but they require large amount of data and high computational power which is not suited for small and m

edium size businesses and that’s where this approach will help solve this problem.  This study focuses on 5 machine learning models- Naïve Bayes, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) and Support Vector Machine (SVM). These machine learning models are going to be trained and evaluated on the Restaurant Reviews dataset. The text data is cleaned, tokenized, stemmed, and converted into numerical features using Bag-of-Words method. Here, two preprocessing methods are compare

d. First is the method that removes the negation words and second is the method that keeps an extended list of negation and sentiment-changing words. Model performance is going to be evaluated based on its accuracy and confusion matrix. We compared the results of both with negation handling and without negation handling. The results show that all models perform better using the second approach, i.e. the method where we kept an extended list of negation and sentiment altering words during preproc

essing. Experimental results shows that keeping negation words improves accuracy across all models from 2% to 9.5%. Out of 5 models, Random Forest performed the best, giving highest accuracy across all models, i.e. 83.5%, followed by Logistic Regression (80.5%), SVM (78.5%), Naïve Bayes (73.5%) and KNN (68%). This confirms that preserving negation words helps models better understand sentiment and improves classification accuracy. This approach provides a simple yet effective improvement for pra

ctical sentiment analysis applications.
Abstract -
Natural Language Processing (NLP) is a significant domain within Artificial Intelligence that enables machines to understand and interpret human language. It has a wide range of applications in the modern world, among which sentiment analysis is one of the most prominent. Sentiment analysis involves identifying and interpreting the emotional tone behind textual data and is widely used in areas such as customer reviews, opinion mining, and recomm

endation systems. This task is commonly performed using machine learning techniques.
Traditional machine learning approaches for sentiment analysis often rely on the Bag-of-Words (BoW) model, which converts textual data into numerical representations. During preprocessing, common stopwords such as “is” and “the” are typically removed to reduce noise. However, this process also eliminates important negation words such as “not,” “no,” and “never.” The removal of such words can significantly alter

the meaning of a sentence and lead to incorrect sentiment classification. For example, the sentence “Food is not good” may be reduced to “Food good,” thereby changing the sentiment from negative to positive.
This chapter investigates the impact of retaining negation-related words on the performance of sentiment classification models. While advanced models such as transformer-based architectures and BERT are capable of capturing such contextual nuances, they require large datasets and substantial

computational resources, making them less suitable for small and medium-sized applications. Therefore, this study proposes a simpler yet effective preprocessing strategy to address this limitation.
The study evaluates five machine learning models: Naïve Bayes, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Support Vector Machine (SVM). These models are trained and tested using the Restaurant Reviews dataset. The text data undergoes preprocessing steps including cleaning, tok

enization, stemming, and feature extraction using the Bag-of-Words approach. Two preprocessing strategies are compared: one that removes negation words and another that preserves an extended list of negation and sentiment-modifying terms.
Model performance is evaluated using accuracy and confusion matrices. The experimental results indicate that all models perform better when negation words are retained. Accuracy improvements ranging from 2% to 9.5% are observed across all models. Among them, Ra

ndom Forest achieves the highest accuracy of 83.5%, followed by Logistic Regression (80.5%), Support Vector Machine (78.5%), Naïve Bayes (73.5%), and KNN (68%).
These findings demonstrate that preserving negation words enhances the model’s ability to correctly interpret sentiment, leading to improved classification performance. This approach provides a simple, efficient, and practical solution for enhancing sentiment analysis systems, particularly in scenarios with limited computational resource
